questions.question: What does the 'inline' axis refer to in CSS Logical Properties?
options: The direction text flows within a line
questions.explanation: The inline axis is the direction text flows within a line. In horizontal writing modes (like English), it's horizontal. In vertical writing modes, it's vertical.

questions.question: Which logical property replaces the physical property 'width'?
questions.explanation: inline-size replaces width. It defines the size along the inline axis (horizontal in LTR, vertical in vertical writing modes).

questions.question: What does margin-inline: auto do in a LTR context?
options: Sets top and bottom margins to auto
questions.explanation: margin-inline: auto centers the element along the inline axis — horizontally in LTR, same as margin: 0 auto but writing-mode aware.

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct logical equivalent for 'border-top'?
questions.explanation: border-block-start replaces border-top. The block axis runs perpendicular to the inline axis (vertical in LTR).

questions.question: In an RTL context (dir='rtl'), what does 'border-inline-start' correspond to?
questions.explanation: In RTL, the inline start is on the right side (reading starts on the right), so border-inline-start affects the right border.

questions.question: What is the logical equivalent of 'text-align: left'?
questions.explanation: text-align: start replaces text-align: left. In LTR it aligns left, in RTL it aligns right, adapting automatically.

questions.question: Which property pair correctly replaces 'left' and 'right' in positioning?
questions.explanation: inset-inline-start replaces left and inset-inline-end replaces right for positioned elements.

questions.question: What does 'block-size' replace?
questions.explanation: block-size replaces height. It defines the size along the block axis (vertical in LTR, horizontal in vertical writing modes).

questions.question: In a vertical writing mode (writing-mode: vertical-rl), what does 'inline-size' control?
questions.explanation: In vertical writing modes, the inline axis runs vertically, so inline-size controls the height of the element.

questions.question: Which property would you use for a top-left corner radius that adapts in RTL?
questions.explanation: border-start-start-radius targets the corner at the start of the block axis and start of the inline axis — top-left in LTR, top-right in RTL.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'inset' shorthand property?
options: Sets all four inset properties at once (top, right, bottom, left)
questions.explanation: The inset shorthand sets all four inset-* properties at once, similar to how 'top', 'right', 'bottom', 'left' work but with logical equivalents.

questions.question: What does 'overflow-inline' control?
options: Overflow on the inline axis (horizontal in LTR)
Overflow on the block axis (vertical in LTR)
questions.explanation: overflow-inline controls overflow on the inline axis, replacing overflow-x in most cases.

questions.question: Which CSS at-rule can you use to provide fallbacks for older browsers without logical property support?
questions.explanation: @supports (feature query) can detect if a browser supports a specific CSS property, allowing you to provide fallbacks: @supports (margin-inline-start: 0) { ... }

questions.question: What is the main advantage of using logical properties for internationalization?
options: Layouts automatically adapt to different writing modes and directions
questions.explanation: Logical properties make layouts writing-mode-aware, automatically adapting to LTR, RTL, and vertical scripts without duplicating CSS rules.

questions.question: Which of the following does NOT have a logical property equivalent?
questions.explanation: The 'float' property does not have a logical equivalent yet. You typically use Flexbox or Grid instead for layout.

questions.question: What is the default value of 'writing-mode' in CSS?
questions.explanation: The default writing mode is horizontal-tb: text flows horizontally, blocks stack top-to-bottom. This is the standard for most Western scripts.

questions.question: In RTL, what happens to 'padding-inline: 0 20px'?
options: Padding is 0 on left, 20px on right
Padding is 0 on right, 20px on left
questions.explanation: padding-inline with two values assigns the first to inline-start and second to inline-end. In RTL, start is right and end is left, so 0 on right, 20px on left.

questions.question: How do you center an element horizontally using logical properties?
questions.explanation: margin-inline: auto centers the element along the inline axis, which is the horizontal direction in LTR writing modes.

questions.question: Which browser was the first to support most CSS Logical Properties?
questions.explanation: Firefox 41+ (2015) was the earliest browser to support logical properties for margin, padding, and border. Chrome added support later in version 69.

questions.question: What is a recommended strategy for migrating an existing project to use logical properties?
options: Rewrite all CSS files at once
Start with text-align, then padding/margin, then sizing and borders incrementally
Only use logical properties for new features, never update existing code
Use JavaScript to convert all properties automatically
questions.explanation: Incremental migration is recommended: start with simple changes like text-align, then move to padding/margin, then sizing and borders. This allows testing at each step.
